original_hypothesis.title: Kernel policy deactivation can leave stale module permissions due to dynamic requestPermissions() symmetry failure
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Kernel._deactivatePolicy calls policy.requestPermissions() at deactivation time to decide what to revoke. If a policy’s requestPermissions output can differ over time (e.g., due to upgrade/migration patterns, selector changes in modules, or a malicious policy), some permissions granted at activation could remain enabled after deactivation. This creates a latent privilege surface where an inactive policy can still call permissioned module functions (including upgrades/freeze/whitelist toggles), enabling unexpected protocol control paths.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While exploitation likely requires executor/admin-level involvement (LOW realism), the resulting operational failure mode is severe: permission matrix drift that is hard to reason about and may enable unauthorized module calls by supposedly inactive policies.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _deactivatePolicy fetches Permissions[] again from policy_.requestPermissions() instead of storing granted set at activation

preconditions: A policy’s requestPermissions() output differs between activation and deactivation (due to code change, proxy pattern, or malicious behavior)
Policy was previously activated (permissions granted) and later deactivated
Module.permissioned checks rely solely on Kernel.modulePermissions mapping (as implemented)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Kernel does not persist the granted permission set; it recomputes on deactivation
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During policy deactivation / after deactivation when stale permissions remain

operational_questions: Are any policies upgradeable or able to change requestPermissions outputs post-activation?
Can module upgrades change selectors such that permission revocation mismatches occur?
Is there monitoring for modulePermissions drift vs expected state?
validation_requirements: Create a test policy whose requestPermissions varies and confirm stale permissions remain
Audit all in-scope policies to confirm requestPermissions is immutable/static
Consider persisting granted permissions in Kernel at activation time

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Arrange for activation with a set of permissions P1
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Cause requestPermissions to return P2 at deactivation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: After deactivation, use remaining P1 permissions to call sensitive module functions

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Possible unauthorized calls into modules by inactive policies
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Unexpected upgrades/freeze/whitelist changes from dormant contracts
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium—requires manual permission cleanup or kernel migration

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: modulePermissions for deactivated policy remaining true
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Calls into modules from policies that are not active
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Policy deactivation followed by unexpected module calls

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
